statement.markdown:
Implement the quick sort algorithm to sort an array of integers.

However, the implementation contains a logical bug.
You should find and fix the bug to make the code work correctly.
statement.inputFormat: Input is given as an array of integers, e.g. [5, 2, 9, 1, 7]
statement.outputFormat: Return a sorted array in ascending order
statement.notes:
Time complexity: O(n log n)
Space complexity: O(log n)

examples.explanation: A sample input and its corresponding output.

hints.text: The bug is related to the partitioning step of the quick sort algorithm.

editorial.title: Correcting the Quick Sort Implementation
editorial.intuition: Quick sort is a divide-and-conquer algorithm that selects a 'pivot' element from the array and partitions the other elements into two sub-arrays, according to whether they are less than or greater than the pivot.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach to quick sort would be to use the insertion sort algorithm for each sub-array, which has a time complexity of O(n^2).
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves using the 'Lomuto' partition scheme, which is an optimized version of the standard 'Hoare' partition scheme.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness relies on the fact that the pivot element is chosen in a way that allows the other elements to be partitioned correctly.
editorial.dryRun: Step-by-step example run of the corrected implementation.
